content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 14, 2015

Jerry Shefren
Member Sequoia Healthcare District Board
30 Valencia Ct.
Portola Valley CA 94028

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-15-237

Dear Mr. Shefren:

       This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

       Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090. Further, the Commission does not provide advice relating to past conduct;
you should not construe this letter as advice about any conduct that may have already taken place.
(See Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).)

QUESTION

       Does the Act prohibit you, a Healthcare District Board member, from participating in
decisions about a nonprofit health clinic in the District where you serve as a volunteer physician?

CONCLUSION

       No. Conflicts of interest under the Act are based on financial effects. Because you are an
unpaid volunteer for Samaritan House Clinic, a nonprofit, you do not have a financial interest in the
clinic as discussed below.

FACTS

       Since 2014, you have served on the Board of the Sequoia Healthcare District, which collects
property taxes to fund health care-related projects in central and southern San Mateo County. The
Board is responsible for operating and maintaining health facilities within the District, and has the
power to create and enforce policies governing the District and all of its facilities.

____________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-15-237
Page No. 2

The Samaritan House is a registered 501(c)(3) public benefit corporation in San Mateo that
provides free services to community members in need, including a free medical clinic. For over ten
years, the Sequoia Healthcare District has provided the clinic with approximately $700,000 per year
in financial assistance. As a member of the District's Board, you have authority to participate in
decisions concerning the clinic, including whether the District should authorize funding for the
clinic.

You are also a retired physician, and volunteer at Samaritan House Clinic half a day per
week. You receive no compensation from the clinic or Samaritan House.

Since you began volunteering for Samaritan House, you have recused yourself from any
Board votes or actions regarding the clinic to avoid any potential conflicts of interest. The issue is
now whether, as a Board member, you may vote on matters concerning the Samaritan House Clinic
without giving rise to a conflict of interest.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. You are a public official under the Act because you are a Board
member of the Sequoia Healthcare District, a public agency. (See e.g., Rubin Advice Letter, No. A-
14-094.)

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's
interests. (Section 87103.) Section 87103 of the Act lists several types of financial interests that can
give rise to a conflict of interest, four of which are most relevant to your situation:

• Source of Income – A public official has a financial interest in any source of
income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within
12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

• Business Entity – A public official has a financial interest in any business
entity in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

• Personal Finances – A public official has a financial interest in his or her
personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family. This is
known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.)

• Gift – A public official has a financial interest in any donor of a gift or gifts
aggregating two hundred fifty dollars or more in value, if the gift is provided
or promised to the official within 12 months prior to a governmental decision.
(Section 87103(e).)

File No. A-15-237
Page No. 3

Sources of Income and Gifts: You are a part-time volunteer physician at Samaritan House clinic, and you receive no compensation or reportable gifts in exchange for your services. Thus, Samaritan House clinic is not an economic interest as a source of income under Section 87103(c).

Business Positions: Section 82005 defines "business entity" as "any organization or enterprise operated for a profit, including but not limited to a proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or association." Samaritan House is a nonprofit organization, and the clinic is an arm of Samaritan House. Because both entities are nonprofit organizations, neither qualifies as a "business entity" under Section 87103(d). We therefore find that your volunteer work for Samaritan House is not a financial interest within the meaning of Section 87103 and does not give rise to a conflict of interest under the Act.

Personal Finances. Next, we must determine whether participating in decisions about the clinic would have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on your personal finances under the "personal financial effects" rule. Regulation 18702.5(a) provides: "A personal financial effect means the financial effect of a governmental decision on the personal finances of a public official or his or her immediate family. The financial effect is material if the official or the official's immediate family member will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision." In this case, however, there are no facts to suggest that the decision will have any financial effect on you or your immediate family's personal finances.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-6421.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Toren A. Lewis
Counsel, Legal Division

TAL:jgl

Does the Act prohibit you, a Healthcare District Board member, from participating in 
decisions about a nonprofit health clinic in the District where you serve as a volunteer physician?

unpaid volunteer for Samaritan House Clinic, a nonprofit, you do not have a financial interest in the
